A review of approaches for submarine landslide-tsunami hazard identification and assessment Jean H.M. Roger a,*, Suzanne Bull a, Sally J. Watson b,c, Christof Mueller a, Jess I.T. Hillman a, Andrea Wolter a, Geoffroy Lamarche d, William Power a, Emily Lane b, Susi Woelz b, Sam Davidson b a GNS Science, 1 Fairway Drive, Lower Hutt, New Zealand b National Institute of Water and Atmospheric Research (NIWA), Wellington, New Zealand c Institute of Marine Science, The University of Auckland, Auckland, New Zealand d School of Environment, The University of Auckland, Auckland, New Zealand A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: Tsunami Submarine landslides Geoscience New Zealand Aotearoa Numerical simulations Hazard assessment PTHA Scenario databases Susceptibility mapping Data science A B S T R A C T Submarine landslides can generate destructive tsunamis. Yet their recurrence intervals and tsunamigenic mechanisms are poorly understood, hampering quantification of global exposure and risk. With growing coastal populations and climatic changes, the impacts of tsunami hazards will increase, and will disproportionately affect peoples in underdeveloped countries (e.g., Small Island Developing States). Specific hazard characteristics of submarine landslide-tsunami include the potential for: short tsunami travel times, highly directional waves, locally extreme wave amplitudes and lack of forewarning. Probabilistic tsunami hazard assessments (PTHA) have emerged as the gold standard for tsunami hazard quantification, enabling science understanding to be translated into policy and decision making. However, our knowledge gaps and research challenges largely prohibit PTHA for submarine landslide sources. and lack of forewarning. Probabilistic tsunami hazard assessments (PTHA) have emerged as the gold standard for tsunami hazard quantification, enabling science understanding to be translated into policy and decision making. However, our knowledge gaps and research challenges largely prohibit PTHA for submarine landslide sources. In this review, we reference Aotearoa New Zealand, as a western Pacific Island nation vulnerable to tsunami hazards, to illustrate the fragmentary nature of the evidence for, and perceived threat of submarine landslide-tsunami typical of many regions globally. We present an overview of geoscience approaches for the identification and assessment of submarine landslide-tsunami hazards, which include con- struction of submarine landslide databases, source-specific numerical tsunami simulations, successful approaches to submarine landslide-specific PTHA, submarine landslide susceptibility mapping and emerging data science techniques. We recommend a sequence of logical and accessible steps for enhanced submarine landslide iden- tification and hazard assessment aimed at geoscience disciplines. Our recommendations promote strategic ap- proaches to future data collection and enhanced multi-disciplinary collaboration between marine geoscientists, and tsunami, hydrodynamic, hazard and risk specialists. 1. Introduction Society is often underprepared for destructive tsunami events, despite decades of disaster risk reduction activities. Tsunami risk will increase along with growing population concentrations in coastal re- gions, particularly within the low-elevation coastal zone (i.e., altitude <10 m). It was estimated that 625 million people (about 10.9 % of the World’s population) lived below 10 m elevation in 2000 (Neumann et al., 2015), a number that is forecast to reach as many as 949 million people by 2030, with most of those people living in underdeveloped countries. Historically, the focus of tsunami research has been on earthquake sources as they cause ~80 % of known tsunamis globally (Harbitz et al., 2014) (Fig. 1). Tsunamigenic landslides can occur in differing settings, including entirely underwater, partially submerged, or initiating terrestrially and impacting a water body such as the sea, a fjord, or a lake (e.g., Harbitz et al., 2006; Løvholt et al., 2015; Takabatake et al., 2020). The highest tsunami run-up ever recorded is attributed to a landslide that initiated on land and impacted an embayment (534 m in Lituya Bay, Alaska 1958; Miller, 1960). These are of particular concern due to the potential for large volume events (i.e., >100’s–1000’s km3) to generate regional tsunami (e.g., Masson et al., 2006; Bondevik et al., 2005) and the po- tential for occurring without warning. Submarine landslides occurring on continental slopes can represent “local” tsunami sources (defined in Aotearoa New Zealand as having less than 1 h propagation time between the source region and the New Zealand coast; Power, 2013) which allow less time to predict hazard impacts and make emergency management decisions compared to more distant sources. Submarine landslides occurring on continental slopes can represent “local” tsunami sources (defined in Aotearoa New Zealand as having less than 1 h propagation time between the source region and the New Zealand coast; Power, 2013) which allow less time to predict hazard impacts and make emergency management decisions compared to more distant sources. Hazard characteristics of submarine landslide tsunami may include: (1) Rapid arrival time or short “tsunami travel time” (TTT: time between wave generation and arrival at coastline) due to high potential for submarine landslides to originate on continental slopes close to coast- lines; (2) Highly directional tsunami waves, which can lead to focussed “corridors” of waves impacting sections of coastline (Ward, 2001; Walters et al., 2006); (3) Large wave amplitudes that can locally exceed those from co-seismic tsunamis as submarine landslides may displace the water column vertically many times more in comparison to fault ruptures (Okal and Synolakis, 2003). This effect can lead to locally extreme runup heights at nearby coasts (e.g., Tappin et al., 2008); (4) Lack of forewarning. While key modern examples of submarine landslide-tsunami have involved submarine landslides triggered by earthquakes (e.g., Tappin et al., 2008; Sepúlveda et al., 2020), there are few ways to realise that a tsunamigenic submarine landslide has been generated in real-time, leading to unexpected wave arrival at coastlines. 1.1. Marine and Petroleum Geology 162 (2024) 106729 3 Probabilistic tsunami hazard and risk assessments (PTHA and PTRA, respectively) offer structured frameworks and procedures that allow key elements and uncertainties to be weighted and traced (e.g., Power, 2013). Whereas deterministic methods consider the impact of single scenarios or specific parameter values, probabilistic (or stochastic) models consider a range of possible scenarios, their likelihood and associated impacts. Because of this, PTHA are becoming the standard basis for tsunami hazard assessment globally (Grezio et al., 2017; Beh- rens et al., 2021). The infrequency of tsunami events remains intrinsi- cally challenging, however, and even more so for rare submarine landslide sources. Significant knowledge gaps and research challenges around submarine landslide sources mean that they are rarely included in regional or national tsunami hazard and risk assessments. standard basis for tsunami hazard assessment globally (Grezio et al., 2017; Beh- rens et al., 2021). The infrequency of tsunami events remains intrinsi- cally challenging, however, and even more so for rare submarine landslide sources. Significant knowledge gaps and research challenges around submarine landslide sources mean that they are rarely included in regional or national tsunami hazard and risk assessments. Given that scientific information is the basis of informed decision making and public awareness, this article aims to: (1) Bring greater awareness to the danger posed by submarine landslide–tsunami using Aotearoa New Zealand in the southwestern Pacific as a case study; (2) Encourage further research aimed at better understanding exposure to submarine landslide-tsunami globally; (3) Provide accessible strategies as next steps for improving submarine landslides identification and risk assessment; and (4) Promote collaborations between science disciplines (i.e., marine geoscientists, sedimentologists, physicists, tsunami scien- tists, data scientists, social scientists, hazard and risk scientists) neces- sary to achieve risk reduction. Tsunami generation by submarine landslides Tsunami may be generated by submarine landslides through energy transfer, as the displacement of material at the seafloor results in displacement of the overlying water column. There is a complex coupling between landslide volume, motion, material properties and water surface deformation (Fig. 3; e.g., Fritz, 2002; Fritz et al., 2003; Harbitz et al., 2006; Løvholt et al., 2015). In comparison to seismic ruptures, submarine landslides typically produce relatively short wavelength, highly dispersive waves primarily because of radial spreading (i.e., a small submarine landslide can be viewed as a point source in comparison to a linear fault rupture; Watts et al., 2003; Grilli and Watts, 2005; Enet and Grilli, 2007; Tappin et al., 2008). Submarine landslide tsunami can be locally higher amplitude than earthquake tsunami as the energy available for the tsunami is proportional to the square of the displacement of the seabed (Okal and Synolakis, 2003). A submarine landslide moves less material globally than a fault rupture; however, it can locally displace the seafloor many times more vertically than a fault displacement, resulting in a comparable amount of tsunami energy (Okal and Synolakis, 2003). The motion of the frontal mass of the submarine landslide generates a positive amplitude wave propagating in the direction of submarine landslide

A submarine landslide moves less material globally than a fault rupture; however, it can locally displace the seafloor many times more vertically than a fault displacement, resulting in a comparable amount of tsunami energy (Okal and Synolakis, 2003). The motion of the frontal mass of the submarine landslide generates a positive amplitude wave propagating in the direction of submarine landslide motion (Fig. 3; Sue et al., 2011). The main component of im- pulse wave generation occurs as the water surface is drawn down above the site of submarine landslide initiation (negative amplitude wave), which “rebounds” and creates a large positive amplitude wave, followed by smaller trailing waves, propagating radially towards, and away from the source area (Fig. 3A; Enet and Grilli, 2007; Sue et al., 2011; Tadepalli and Synolakis, 1994; Yavari-Ramshe and Ataie-Ashtiani, 2016). Submarine landslide initial volume and initial acceleration exert first order control over wave magnitude (Ward, 2001; Lee, 2009; Kirby et al., 2016; Løvholt et al., 2015). Both the initial acceleration and maximum velocity achieved by a submarine landslide are governed by material density, submergence depth, and slope angle (Haugen et al., 2005; Grilli and Watts, 2005). Rotational “slump”-type landslides are consequently thought to be more effective wave generators (e.g., Matsumoto et al., 2003; Tappin et al., 2008) versus more shallowly-detaching “slab slide” or “grain flow” type failures. Submergence depth influences the land- slide Froude number (ratio of landslide velocity to impulse wave celerity; see Harbitz et al., 2006). Higher Froude numbers indicate more efficient wave generation (Harbitz et al., 2014), although tsunamis can still be generated for lower Froude numbers (Whittaker et al., 2015). Abrupt deceleration of the landslide is also thought to contribute to larger

wave generators (e.g., Matsumoto et al., 2003; Tappin et al., 2008) versus more shallowly-detaching “slab slide” or “grain flow” type failures. Higher Froude numbers indicate more efficient wave generation (Harbitz et al., 2014), although tsunamis can still be generated for lower Froude numbers (Whittaker et al., 2015). Abrupt deceleration of the landslide is also thought to contribute to larger wave heights (Sue et al., 2011; Løvholt et al., 2015; Power et al., 2016). Previous studies have suggested that submarine landslide ge- ometry may influence wave generation, as for a given volume, thicker landslides lead to higher wave amplitudes (e.g., Ward, 2001; Grilli and Watts, 2005; Haugen et al., 2005; Løvholt et al., 2005; Harbitz et al., 2006; Ataie-Ashtiani and Naja-Jilani, 2008). Flow transformation (i.e., the process by which submarine landslides transform into other flow types such as turbidity or density flows; Nemec, 1990; Martinsen, 1994; Eyles and Eyles, 2000; Strachan, 2008; Joanne et al., 2013) may also be a factor in controlling wave amplitude. Submarine landslides showing a longer runout distance have shown larger maximum wave amplitudes than examples with shorter runout distances (Haugen et al., 2005), but transformation is also linked to energy loss and predominantly occurs as the landslide enters deeper water, thus inducing only minor influence. Modern events have demonstrated the link between submarine landslide and tsunami, including at the northern coastline of Papua New Guinea in 1998. A Mw 7.1 earthquake was followed 10–25 min later by a series of waves reaching a maximum height of 10–15 m along a 40 km coastal stretch, resulting in 2200 deaths (Davies et al., 2003). Modern events have demonstrated the link between submarine landslide and tsunami, including at the northern coastline of Papua New Guinea in 1998. A Mw 7.1 earthquake was followed 10–25 min later by a series of waves reaching a maximum height of 10–15 m along a 40 km coastal stretch, resulting in 2200 deaths (Davies et al., 2003). While the tsunami was initially attributed to the earthquake, subsequent analysis showed that the observed wave characteristics were more consistent with a submarine landslide source (e.g., Tappin et al., 1999; Heinrich et al., 2001; Synolakis et al., 2002; Lynett et al., 2003; Tappin et al., 2008). Geophysical surveys revealed evidence of multiple submarine landslides nearby, including a recent “slump” type failure with an esti- mated volume of 6.4 km3 in ~1600 m water depth (e.g., Synolakis et al., 2002; Tappin et al., 2008). The 1929 Mw 7.2 earthquake on the southwestern Grand Banks of Newfoundland was followed by waves with a maximum run up height of 13 m at the Newfoundland coast, resulting in 28 deaths and severe damage to property and infrastructure (Murty, 1977; Piper et al., 1988; Evans, 2001). Waves were also recorded as far away as mainland Portugal (Fine et al., 2005; Løvholt et al., 2019). Turbidity flows generated by the event broke a series of sub-sea telecommunication cables and travelled a total distance of 1000 km at speeds of 60–100 km/h over 4–11 h (Heezen and Ewing, 1952; Kuenen, 1952; Evans, 2001). This tsunami has been attributed to submarine landslides (Murty, 1977; Clague, 2001; Fine et al., 2005), with evidence of widespread surficial slope failures, estimated at a total of 150–175 km3 (Piper et al., 1988, 1999) later discovered. the event broke a series of sub-sea telecommunication cables and travelled a total distance of 1000 km at speeds of 60–100 km/h over 4–11 h (Heezen and Ewing, 1952; Kuenen, 1952; Evans, 2001). This tsunami has been attributed to submarine landslides (Murty, 1977; Clague, 2001; Fine et al., 2005), with evidence of widespread surficial slope failures, estimated at a total of 150–175 km3 (Piper et al., 1988, 1999) later discovered. A more recent study also identified a 560 km3 “complex slump” in the area (Schulten et al., 2019). The most intensely studied prehistoric tsunamigenic submarine landslide is the ~8150 BP Storegga Slide of western Norway (e.g., Haflidason et al., 2004, 2005; Bryn et al., 2005; Kim et al., 2019). The slide mobilized 3200 km3 of material, which has been linked to contemporaneous inundations of up to 20 m run-up heights in the sur- rounding region (Bondevik et al., 2005). Interestingly, this region ex- periences low levels of seismicity due to isostatic glacial adjustment of the Scandinavian Region after the last glacial maximum ice-sheet loss (Shiddiqi et al., 2022). A dynamic trigger (e.g., an earthquake) is still postulated however, with slope failure strongly preconditioned by rapid deposition of sediments associated with glacial and interglacial climatic cycles (Solheim et al., 2005). 1.3. Major challenges for submarine landslide-tsunami hazard assessment Previous works have summarized major knowledge gaps for sub- marine landslides (e.g., Huhn et al., 2019) and for tsunami hazard and risk assessment (e.g., Behrens et al., 2021; Sassa et al., 2023). (2021) present the results of a panel of 50 experts who identified the following gaps for all submarine landslide sources: (1) Lack of un- derstanding and likelihoods for submarine landslide volumes; (2) Dif- ferences between onshore and submarine landslides; (3) Limited constraints on landslide dynamics and material behaviour; (4) Limited availability of benchmarks; and (5) limited past events to inform hazard models. They concluded that the most important, yet challenging research gap, is the lack of understanding and likelihoods for tsunami- genic landslide volumes and occurrence rates. In the following section, we provide an overview of specific elements as a basis for examining ways to address them in later sections. 1.3.1. Lack of tsunamigenic landslide volumes and occurrence rate Comprehensive PTHA relies on establishment of magnitude fre- quency curves which is exceptionally challenging for submarine land- slides. Documenting past submarine landslides from the modern seafloor relies on the acquisition and availability of high-quality, extensive bathymetric data coverage that, ideally, is supported and/or calibrated by subsurface geophysical data (e.g., sub-bottom profiles, seismic reflection data) and sediment samples. Several regional and national J.H.M. Roger et al. Marine and Petroleum Geology 162 (2024) 106729 5 submarine landslide databases have been compiled in recent decades (see Section 3.1). Such endeavors essentially provide past submarine landslide magnitudes and can reveal spatial relationships to geomorphic domains and potential triggering mechanisms (e.g., Watson et al., 2020; Gamboa et al., 2021). A further challenge is related to the natural preservation of submarine landslide headscarps and deposits. Watson et al. (2020) showed that fewer than 1 % of documented submarine landslide head- scarps offshore eastern New Zealand could be associated with a deposit, which inhibits accurate assessment of submarine landslide prevalence. Return rates for large volume submarine landslides (hundreds to thousands of km3) are very low (e.g., every 100 ka on the mid- Norwegian Continental Margin; Solheim et al., 2005). Various pre- conditioning factors may prime slopes for failure, including over- pressure due to rapid sediment deposition (Stigall and Dugan, 2010), sea level change (Piper et al., 2003; Maslin et al., 2004; Lee, 2009; Lee et al., 2010; Leynaud et al., 2009), and weak layers (e.g., Wu et al., 2023). When submarine slopes are prone to failure; an earthquake is a likely trigger mechanism (e.g., Micallef et al., 2012; Mountjoy et al., 2014), however, heavy rainfalls (e.g., Pope et al., 2017; Clare et al., 2018), or anthropogenic origin (human-induced landslides [HIL]; e.g., Jaboyedoff et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020), are also implicated. Often, such factors are weakly tested, and impacted by a lack of known submarine landslide ages and age uncertainties, many of which have uncertainty intervals too large to be directly assigned to a particular sea level condition (Urlaub et al., 2015). Uncertainty is mainly introduced due to the location of dating samples above and below the submarine landslide deposit (see Urlaub et al., 2015, for a summary of sampling strategies and dating techniques), and to a lesser extent, the “marine reservoir effect” which causes radiocarbon ages of marine samples to appear several hundred years older than coeval terrestrial samples (Stuiver and Braziunas, 1993). Deeply buried (i.e., older) submarine landslides are generally under-sampled, while some margins or regions are preferen- tially studied relative to others (Urlaub et al., 2015). Also, the level of preservation of the landslide scars and deposits is dependent on the erosion vs. sedimentation processes and could introduce a non-negligible size bias in the individual landslide identification (e.g., Chaytor et al., 2009). Because of these collective biases and un- certainties, along with the very small number of well-dated events globally and particularly for small events, sampled submarine landslide ages appear temporally random (Hunt et al., 2013; Urlaub et al., 2015; Clare et al., 2014; Pope et al., 2015) which prohibits confident corre- lation to specific triggers or precursor events. Earthquake ground shaking is the predominant triggering mecha- nism cited or inferred for submarine landslides and demonstrated by modern examples such as Grand Banks in 1929, Papua New Guinea, 1998 (Section 1.2), and more recently, Palu, Indonesia in 2018 (Carvajal et al., 2019). Modelling and statistical studies suggest that some, but not all, Mw 7.0 and above earthquakes can trigger numerous submarine landslides, many of them mobilizing small volume materials (Watts and Borrero, 2001; V¨olker et al., 2011). We also note direct evidence of submarine landslides triggered by storms (Zhang et al., 2016), for example 14 submarine landslides were triggered during Typhoon Mor- akot in Taiwan, 2009 (Lin, 2015). Finally, gas hydrate dissociation can trigger submarine landslides via the reduction of the cohesion between the sediment particles of the storage reservoir (e.g., Zhang et al., 2021). However, there is also growing evidence of submarine landslides initi- ating in the absence of known triggers (Fan et al., 2020; Clare et al., in press). There is, however, evidence of fewer submarine landslides since establishment of the current sea-level highstand at 6 ka, than during the period of sea-level rise since the Last Glacial Maximum (LGM) from ~21 ka (Urlaub et al., 2015). Continental slopes are expected to be relatively stable during high, stable sea levels, as sediment delivery is reduced, sediment stress conditions equilibrate and post-glacial seismicity de- creases. Urlaub et al. (2015) therefore suggests that recent and modern submarine landslides occurrences are more likely to be caused by sea level-independent factors such as weak layers and

(2015) therefore suggests that recent and modern submarine landslides occurrences are more likely to be caused by sea level-independent factors such as weak layers and tectonic seismicity. 1.3.2. Limited knowledge on landslide dynamics and material behaviour The dynamic processes of submarine landslides are poorly under- stood because we lack direct observations in situ at full scale. For example, we have very little empirical information on initial accelera- tion of submarine landslides, which is a first order control on tsunami- genesis (see Section 1.2). To provide a ‘fix’ for this challenge, Heller et al. (2009) and Heller and Hager (2014) developed an empirical method in Swiss lakes. It is the most broadly applied method to estimate generic displacement wave parameters based on laboratory tests. It provides estimates of displacement wave height, amplitude, period, length, run-up height, average discharge, etc. Maximum runout and mobility have been shown to be influential (De Blasio et al., 2006a; Lee, 2009), yet the mechanisms that govern flow transformation are not well understood. The initial density of the un- stable mass may be important, (Lee et al., 1993) along with hydro- planing, which occurs when a thin, underlying water layer reduces friction between the failed mass and the seafloor as it travels downslope (De Blasio et al., 2006a). The initial density of the un- stable mass may be important, (Lee et al., 1993) along with hydro- planing, which occurs when a thin, underlying water layer reduces friction between the failed mass and the seafloor as it travels downslope (De Blasio et al., 2006a). The internal shear strain of the unstable mass has control over emplacement as well (Hungr et al., 2005; Di Maio et al., 2013; Stead and Eberhardt, 2013; Yerro et al., 2016; Steventon et al., 2019), and may be distributed throughout the mass profile or concen- trated on discrete shear planes within the mass. Conversely, seafloor erosion and entrainment of material may occur where the landslide exerts a shear stress higher than the shear resistance of the seabed (De Blasio et al., 2006b). Grilli and Watts (2005), Løvholt et al. (2015), and Kirby et al. (2016) all recommend a more detailed investigation into the role of submarine landslide thickness, runout, and density on tsunami- genic potential. Behrens et al. (2021) also point out that fluid resistance forces (i.e., pressure drag, skin friction, and added mass) could be important (e.g., Harbitz, 1992), but have barely been explored. Poor understanding of landslide dynamics is compounded by the simplistic representation of submarine landslides in numerical models (e.g., Fine et al., 2005; Grilli and Watts, 2005; Hill et al., 2014; Løvholt et al., 2005). The simplest way to represent a submarine landslide is to use a block model, which has been successful in reconciling certain as- pects of observed submarine landslides attributed to “slump type” fail- ures, such as the 1998 Papua New Guinea event (Synolakis et al., 2002; Tappin et al., 2008; see Section 1.2). Such simplified approaches cannot accurately replicate or predict real-world submarine landslide tsunami, where complex dynamics and material behaviour occur (e.g., Elverhøi et al.,

Such simplified approaches cannot accurately replicate or predict real-world submarine landslide tsunami, where complex dynamics and material behaviour occur (e.g., Elverhøi et al., 2005; Talling et al., 2014). Granular flow (e.g., Ma et al., 2015; Yavari-Ramshe and Ataie-Ashtiani, 2017) and multiphase (granular) flow (e.g., Yu and Lee, 2019; Dai et al., 2021) simulations are now more frequently used to reproduce the change of cohesion within a sliding mass and the interactions between the sliding mass and its environment (e.g., the surrounding water). However, current evidence from physical models and numerical studies relating to the influence of landslide type, behaviour and geometry (typically “rigid blocks” versus thinner, more deformable “granular flows”) on wave parameters is divergent (as for the terrestrial cases), and warrants further investigation (e.g., Zweifel, 2004; Panizzo et al., 2005; Ataie-Ashtiani and Nik-Khah, 2008). 2. Hikurangi Margin is dominated by high seismicity and high rates of tectonic deformation up to 47 mm/yr (e.g., Nicol and Wallace, 2007). Continuing south, the subduction margin transitions to continental collision and strike-slip along the Alpine Fault onshore the South Island. The boundary continues offshore to the southwest of the South Island, with a reversed subduction polarity, whereby oceanic crust of the Australian Plate is being subducted obliquely beneath Pacific Plate continental crust at the Puysegur Trench (Gurnis et al., 2019). A domain of low to no tectonic strain, considered to be a passive margin (Litchfield et al., 2014) is located offshore along eastern South Island, from the Chatham Rise southward to the Otago Canyons). In northwestern New Zealand, the margin that borders the Tasman Sea is also considered to be passive, despite modern seismicity originating from extensional offshore faults (Seebeck et al., 2023). To the south, adjacent to the Challenger Plateau, the margin associated with the western South Island is narrow, steep and interrupted by sig- nificant submarine canyons that drain to the deep Tasman Basin (Ste- vens et al., 2021). 2.1. Eastern New Zealand Several examples of submarine landslides from eastern New Zealand have been extensively studied on a site-specific basis, including the Matakaoa Submarine Instability Complex (Fig. 5a; Lamarche et al., 2008) which totals 2000 km3 in volume. The Complex includes the Matakaoa Debris Flow, a 200-km-long mass-transport deposit which resulted from the most recent failure, dated to ca. 38–100 ka ago (Joanne et al., 2013). Joanne (2008) used a numerical model to simulate the potential of smaller failures on the flanks of the Complex to produce tsunami and demonstrated the potential for significant inundation along the coastline of East Cape. The Ruatoria Debris Avalanche (Fig. 5a), offshore of the East Cape, consists of a proximal, up to 200-m-thick, lobe characterized by numerous kilometre-scale megablocks, and a more distal debris flow deposit extending a total distance of 100 km (Collot et al., 2001). Formation of the Ruatoria re-entrant and associated failure deposits occurred as a result of multi-phase collapse of the outer trench-wedge slope in response to seamount subduction over the last 2 Myrs (Collot et al., 2001; Davidson, 2022). The Tuaheni Landslide Complex (Fig. 5a) covers 145 km2 (Mountjoy et al., 2009), and is interpreted as a reactivated, slow-moving failure (Mountjoy et al., 2009). It includes units dated from the Holocene to Late Pliocene (i.e., last 11.7 ka–3.6 Ma; Luo et al., 2020). Geotechnical testing on samples from the base of the complex suggested that a hy- pothetical local Mw 8.0 megathrust earthquake would be required to mobilise the sediments (Carey et al., 2022). Movement is likely to be episodic, ceasing when ground shaking reduces or stops. This supports the “slow-moving” morphological interpretation and the hypothesis that submarine landslides of similar morphology may not evolve into cata- strophic, tsunamigenic events. Watson et al. (2020) provides the first regional compilation of sub- marine landslides based on an extensive, compiled bathymetric dataset that spans both active and passive stretches of continental margin (Section 2; Fig. 5a). No correla- tion between estimated landslide volume and a range of preconditioning and triggering factors (including seafloor slope, proximity to faults, gas hydrate stability, and ground shaking) was found. However, the study showed that most submarine landslides occur within canyon systems, with 68 % being within 5 km of canyon thalwegs.

Western New Zealand There is comparatively little bathymetric data available for the western New Zealand margins (Fig. 5a). Knowledge of prehistoric sub- marine landslides is taken from rock outcrop studies (e.g., Strachan, 2002; King et al., 2011; Sharman et al., 2015; Bull et al., 2020) and a large offshore legacy seismic reflection dataset (The New Zealand Exploration Data Pack, 2018; https://www.nzpam.govt.nz/mapsg eoscience/petroleum-datapack/). Regionally, a total of 180 buried submarine landslide deposits have recently been mapped as part of the New Zealand submarine landslides database (Bull et al., in prep.). Most notably, 5 extreme volume submarine landslides (estimates ranging from 377 to 4064 km3), including the largest volume in New Zealand, have been identified in the Deepwater Taranaki Basin (Figs. 4 and 5a, d; Omeru, 2014; Omeru et al., 2016; Bull et al., 2020). The submarine landslides have occurred throughout the Plio-Pleistocene (i.e., last 5 Ma) and are linked to high sediment supply in response to an increased plate boundary convergence rate (Kamp et al., 2004; Hansen and Kamp, 2006). Sediments shed from the ancestral Southern Alps were trans- ported north by oceanic current action and built steep, high relief (up to 1500 m) prograding clinoforms at the margin front (Hansen and Kamp, 2002; Anell and Mitkandal, 2017; Salazar et al., 2015), but specific preconditioning factors and triggering mechanisms are poorly under- stood. Submarine landslide-tsunamis in Aotearoa New Zealand Despite its complex geodynamic setting and rich evidence of historic submarine landslides, Aotearoa New Zealand does not have a confirmed tsunami resulting from a submarine landslide. In New Zealand, the National Tsunami Hazard Model (NTHM, ; Power et al., 2022) is the key science product which estimates tsunami heights at the shoreline for return periods of up to 2500 years, and underpins evacuation zone design, land-use planning and risk assessments (Power et al., 2013; Power et al., 2022). The NTHM does not yet include submarine landslide sources of tsunami due to: (1) Perceived infrequency of tsunami events; (2) A lack of specificity in tsunami hazard return rates; (3) Knowledge gaps in scenario-specific studies for large potentially hazardous events; and (4) The hazard posed from these tsunami sources on time frames of up to 2500 years is considered secondary to the hazard from earthquake-generated tsunami (Power, 2013). Here, we provide an overview of established and emerging scientific techniques and approaches that can be employed to better understand submarine landslide-tsunami potential and hazards, and enhanced preparation for future events. 3.1. Building submarine landslide databases Submarine landslide databases can yield spatial distributions and magnitudes of historic submarine landslides and form the basis for sta- tistical analysis to identify preconditioning factors, triggering mecha- nisms, geomorphic relationships and controls on magnitude-frequency distributions (Clare et al., 2019). A minimum requirement is the compilation of all available bathymetric data for a region or area, fol- lowed by morphometric analysis. Morphometric parameters (e.g., scar dimensions, run out distance, volume, directionality etc.) are especially relevant to tsunami generation modelling. Examples of regional com- pilations include the US Atlantic margin (ten Brink et al., 2009; Chaytor et al., 2009; Twichell et al., 2009), the Mediterranean Sea (Camerlenghi et al., 2010; Migeon et al., 2011; Urgeles and Camerlenghi, 2013; Casalbore et al., 2020; Urgeles et al., 2023), Gulf of Mexico (Pampell-- Manis et al., 2016), Australia (Clarke et al., 2019), New Zealand (Watson et al., 2020), the Spanish margins (Le´on et al., 2020), Iberia (Gamboa et al., 2021) and Cascadia (Lenz and Sawyer, 2021; Hill et al., 2020, 2022). This component of the database will enable enhanced long-term understanding of submarine landslide occurrence. The data that supports identification of buried deposits is mostly derived from offshore energy resource exploration. With similar datasets available from many continental margins globally (e.g., see examples in Moscardelli and Wood, 2016), this approach could be applied in many other geographic locations to supplement submarine landslide databases based on seafloor bathymetry. 3.2. Submarine landslide-tsunami source simulations Computational numerical modelling of submarine landslide pro- cesses and the tsunami waves they generate is one of the key approaches to understand the hazard potential of submarine landslides, alongside physical laboratory experiments (e.g., Grilli et al., 2002; Enet et al., 2003; Ma et al., 2015). Numerical models can predict wave behavior, arrival times, coastal wave heights and current velocities. Of particular value to our general understanding are high fidelity back-analyses of real-world events, where both the submarine landslide source parame- ters and tsunami characteristics are well constrained. The Papua New Guinea, 1998 event is one of the best modern examples of this (Tappin et al., 2008). Identification and detailed correlation of paleotsunami deposits along with a wealth of marine geophysical data provided excellent constraints on numerical modelling of the prehistoric Storegga Slide and tsunami (Bondevik et al., 2005). The Papua New Guinea, 1998 event is one of the best modern examples of this (Tappin et al., 2008). Identification and detailed correlation of paleotsunami deposits along with a wealth of marine geophysical data provided excellent constraints on numerical modelling of the prehistoric Storegga Slide and tsunami (Bondevik et al., 2005). An extremely diverse range of numerical and conceptual approaches exists (see Yavari-Ramshe and Ataie-Ashtiani (2016) for an in-depth review) and includes up to four stages including landslide initiation and run out, tsunami generation, tsunami propagation and coastal inundation. Examples of submarine landslide-tsunami modelling studies vary in complexity and have been based on: (1) Shallow Water Equa- tions, (e.g., Harbitz, 1992); (2) Fully nonlinear potential flow (e.g., Grilli and Watts, 2005); (3) Boussinesq equations (Lynett and Liu, 2002); and (4) Navier–Stokes equations (e.g., Heinrich, 1992). The majority of numerical models currently in use for submarine landslide-specific sources have been in continuous development and adapted following initial application to earthquake sources. Examples include: TUNAMI-N2 (Imamura, 1995), MOST (Titov and Gonzalez, 1997), COMCOT (Liu et al., 1998), FUNWAVE (Kirby et al., 1998), Basilisk (formerly Gerris; Popinet, 2003), RiCOM (Walters et al., 2006), NHWAVE (Ma et al., 2012), and HySEA (Macías et al., 2015) numerical models. For more information about the available models, see Kirby et al. (2022) and references therein. Additionally, submarine landslide input parameters considered for modelling include volume, thickness, density, frictional and rheological properties, and initial failure velocity. Masson et al. (2006) notes the reliability of modelled outputs are sensitive to the quality of available input geological data. Given limitations in currently available geological datasets across submarine landslides in the marine environment and the significant investment required to collect additional samples, one avenue to increase our understanding of submarine landslide-tsunami hazards and impacts is to increase the number of submarine landslide source simulations undertaken. While current models are typically developed on a site-specific basis for notable events, moving towards more regional-scale simulations of multiple events, ideally a subset of real-world examples from a submarine landslide database should be used. This approach can provide a range of magnitude distributions, a detailed picture of potential tsunami hazards and reveal insights into sensitivity of submarine landslide tsunami to various parameters including water depth and submarine landslide geometry. 3.3. Probabilistic tsunami hazard assessments PTHA has become a standard approach for assessing tsunami hazard (see Grezio et al., 2017 for a thorough review of PTHA) as it helps to account for both natural randomness (aleatoric variability) of hazardous processes, as well as our incomplete understanding (epistemic uncer- tainty; Grezio et al., 2017). PTHA typically estimates the probability of exceeding specific levels of tsunami intensities, including coastal wave heights, run-up heights and water current velocities (e.g., Power et al., 2013, 2022) for given coastlines or specific sites within a certain period of time. The results are most often communicated as hazard curves, hazard maps or probability maps (Grezio et al., 2017). These output metrics in turn are used for tsunami risk assessment including J.H.M. Although viewed as a standard for assessing tsunami hazard, PTHA can take many forms, be expressed via many different outputs, and lacks standardization (Grezio et al., 2017). Predicting submarine landslide occurrence (susceptibility mapping) Submarine landslide occurrence can be investigated at the regional, local, or site-specific, scale. Regional scale analysis involves heuristic, deterministic, or most commonly statistical methods, investigating the susceptibility of material to mass movement given triggering and pre- conditioning variables. At the local or site-specific scale, more detailed analyses may be completed, including simulations of landslide initiation and runout, material testing, and physics-based analysis of failure initiation and behaviour. Below, we summarise the methods that have been used to investigate submarine landslide occurrence. We summarise approaches, typically used for onshore (earthquake-induced) landslides, in Fig. 8. At a regional scale, landslide susceptibility maps and models are used to predict landslide occurrence and are commonly used in onshore landslide hazard analysis (e.g., Dymond et al., 2006; Frattini et al., 2010; Guzzetti et al., 2006; Huang and Zhao, 2018; Reichenbach et al., 2018; Smith et al., 2021; Massey et al., 2022a,b). They typically employ GIS, multivariate statistical and/or machine-learning methods (see Section 3.6) over regional scales to determine the likelihood of landsliding, and do not consider physical process, behaviour, or material properties. Regional-scale statistical, heuristic and physics-based methods are a common approach onshore because material sampling, drilling data and remote monitoring methods are more expensive, site-specific ap- proaches that are not widely available and spatially constrained. In the offshore context, submarine landslide susceptibility is a rela- tively new endeavour using GIS tools and requires sufficient coverage of high-resolution bathymetry datasets (to derive slope angle, relief, cur- vature, and local slope relief). These approaches require engineering judgement and as- sumptions and/or detailed data on material properties, which are commonly not available or difficult to acquire. Lee and Edwards (1986) were one of the first to suggest a method for evaluation of offshore earthquake-induced slope stability for Alaska and California, using a modified version of the pseudostatic infinite slope method. The infinite slope method calculates an averaged factor of safety, simply expressed as the ratio of the resisting shear strength of material on an assumed failure plane to the driving shear stress due to the self-weight of the slope (Morgenstern, 1967). Pseudostatic analyses are a simplified tool representing earthquakes as a static horizontal and/or vertical load on a slope. More recently, studies have focussed on probabilistic assessments of submarine landslide susceptibility which have the advantage of being more consistent with the needs of PTHA (see Section 3.2). Deep learning, statistical meta models, or surrogate modelling The complexity of modeling cascading hazards, such as an earthquake-submarine landslide-tsunami system, for probabilistic as- sessments is an enormous technical challenge. Any component of this cascade can be simulated in different levels of detail, from highly complex computationally intensive to fast crude approximations. Therefore, in the past, large ensembles of individual hazards have mostly been studied with approximating algorithms that do not allow investigation of complex and important details of the hazard. For tsunami, as a prominent example, Geist (2002) investigates the effects of rupture complexity on the near shore tsunami wavefield but does not consider inundation as this would involve expensive non-linear simu- lations that were probably not feasible at the time. Others reduce the problem to a manageable size of ensembles that might not reach sta- tistical significance (e.g., Mueller et al., 2015)

studied with approximating algorithms that do not allow investigation of complex and important details of the hazard. For tsunami, as a prominent example, Geist (2002) investigates the effects of rupture complexity on the near shore tsunami wavefield but does not consider inundation as this would involve expensive non-linear simu- lations that were probably not feasible at the time. Others reduce the problem to a manageable size of ensembles that might not reach sta- tistical significance (e.g., Mueller et al., 2015) but then include the complex inundation step. Ensembles are a necessary consideration when uncertainties around the source mechanism are high. This is very much the case for potential submarine landslide sources (e.g., Zen- gaffinen-Morris et al., 2022). However, simulating the submarine landslide process in detail is another example of a computationally expensive process and often approximations are used to evaluate sta- tistically relevant ensemble sizes (e.g., Lane et al., 2016). In seismic hazard assessment probabilistic methods are widely established, but commonly based on ground motion prediction equations, which fit vast amounts of observations to empirical curves. This approach avoids the need for simulations altogether, delivers reliable estimates, but is only feasible due to the large number of observations available (Pagani et al., 2014). For the tsunami case, it results in approximations of tsunami waves behavior, because of the strong influences of bathymetry and sources’ orientation. However, for cascading hazard assessment details of the earthquake, submarine landslide and tsunami and their coupling become relevant. Often the number of required simulations is reduced by specifically designed sampling of the parameter-space to find a smaller but repre- sentative sample in the input space. These techniques are commonly referred to as ‘experimental design’ (e.g., Sacks et al., 1989). ‘Active experimental design’, also known as sequential design, uses results that have already been computed to

Often the number of required simulations is reduced by specifically designed sampling of the parameter-space to find a smaller but repre- sentative sample in the input space. These techniques are commonly referred to as ‘experimental design’ (e.g., Sacks et al., 1989). ‘Active experimental design’, also known as sequential design, uses results that have already been computed to select future input points from the parameter space (e.g., Beck and Guillas, 2016). This technique was used to study run-up amplification behind an island by Stefanakis et al. (2014). For further details please also refer to Behrens and Dias (2015). Recent advances in data science and deep machine learning, however, make the realization of complex cascading computational experiments more tangible. There are several different terms for this kind of approach. Apart from deep learning we find terms such as sur- rogate modelling, statistical modelling, statistical emulation (e.g., Beh- rens and Dias, 2015) or meta modelling (e.g., Friedman, 2012). Mostly these approaches try to estimate a multi parameter function that fits a large volume of simulated or measured examples. This is comparable to fitting a mathematical model to a large set of observations (e.g., ground motion prediction equations). The difference being that the data driven approach allows for finding the fit of a large parameter function to the available data which will normally not have any physical meaning or well-defined mathematical form. Statistical models can have hundreds of thousands of parameters. Such models recently have mostly been referred to as deep learning models. Machine learning methodologies have been used to address several aspects of the research surrounding earthquake, landslide or tsunami phenomena. Seismo-acoustic characterisation and monitoring Targeted offshore monitoring is one of the few avenues available to acquire observational data on submarine landslides, but is challenged by deep water, remote locations, unpredictable landslide occurrence and nature, and potentially vast areas where submarine landslides may occur. Logistic and technological barriers also exist, including battery life and data storage issues (see Clare et al., in press, for an extensive review of offshore monitoring of submarine landslides). Recently, short-duration, multi-parameter targeted monitoring studies of turbi- dites have yielded measurements of event frequency, duration, velocity and sediment concentration (e.g., Talling et al., 2023). Onshore, acoustic monitoring of terrestrial landslides has been used for over a decade as an early warning tool after their seismo-acoustic signatures were first recorded unintentionally by passive systems (i.e., long-term, large-scale coverage, such as GeoNet in New Zealand; https://www.geonet.org,nz). Summary & recommendations Probabilistic Tsunami Hazard Assessment (PTHA) has become the gold standard for quantifying tsunami hazard (and following that, assessment of risk), driven mostly by the need to mitigate risk from destructive earthquake-tsunami. However, the lack of known return rates around submarine landslide sources makes PTHA challenging, as the critical input of magnitude-frequency relationships cannot be established. Consequently, it is difficult to give submarine landslide sources the same treatment as earthquake sources. The problem of quantifying submarine landslide return rates is not likely to be solved until: (1) Many more surveys at local or regional scale are carried out to acquire targeted age and/or geotechnical information for past landslide events (particularly smaller landslides) and regular surveys are needed in specific areas to estimate the disappearance time for small events; (2) Submarine landslide dating techniques improve so that margins for error in failure ages are significantly reduced; and (3) Monitoring of in-situ slope conditions and/or direct evidence of submarine landslide pro- cess initiation and dynamic evolution yield new insights into relation- ships between submarine landslides and their preconditioning factors, precursor events and dynamic triggers. Despite the challenges, scientists are increasingly borrowing from more mature practices applied to other natural hazards, including PTHA frameworks developed for earthquake sources and landslide suscepti- bility and process modelling developed for onshore landslides. The task before researchers is how to support increased and enhanced submarine landslide hazard and risk assessment that utilizes the most commonly available datasets and meaningfully quantifies submarine landslide- tsunami hazard against a complex backdrop of cascading hazards, perceived risk or likelihood, and justification for the resources required. We suggest a high level “road map”, framed within a realistic time period of the next 10–20 years, based on series of steps and initiatives that can be taken

for onshore landslides. The task before researchers is how to support increased and enhanced submarine landslide hazard and risk assessment that utilizes the most commonly available datasets and meaningfully quantifies submarine landslide- tsunami hazard against a complex backdrop of cascading hazards, perceived risk or likelihood, and justification for the resources required. We suggest a high level “road map”, framed within a realistic time period of the next 10–20 years, based on series of steps and initiatives that can be taken globally, to identify and assess submarine landslide- related hazards for any region or nation. We focus on logical and accessible actions to support disaster risk reduction measures (summa- rized in Fig. 9). Short term (i.e., next 1–5 years): Identification of past submarine landslide occurrence. This can be achieved via construction of subma- rine landslide databases (Section 3.1) using the framework of Clare et al. (2018) to produce consistent and comparable dataset of submarine landslide parameters. This begins with collation of existing conventional marine geophysical datasets, principally bathymetry – global collation of which is underway via the Seabed 2030 initiative (www. https://se abed2030.org). There is potential value in collating further data types however, including sub-bottom profiles, seismic reflection data and sediment samples (cores). Mid-term (i.e., next 5–10 years): (1) Improvement of simulation techniques, for both tsunami and submarine landslides, including vali- dation through real cases; (2) Transformation into tsunami hazard im- pacts; (3) probabilistic quantification of risk; and (4) integration of AI technologies. The tsunami numerical simu- lation codes for earthquake sources are improving with time, showing more and more robustness and their results are getting closer to reality with the help of significant modern events. However, there is still a need for further real-world examples to provide data of both the source (i.e., landslide parameters) and the tsunami (i.e., observation data and sea- level records) in order to validate the simulation models. Geospatial hazard metrics produced from source-specific numerical models can form the basis of evacuation zone mapping, probabilistic hazard and risk assessment and land use planning. Landslide susceptibility mapping (Section 3.4) offers a validated tool for forecasting submarine landslide occurrence with most relevance to cascading hazard scenarios involving earthquakes and other processes which trigger submarine landslides. Results can also be used for further tsunamis source modelling. Data science approaches (Section 3.5) can be applied to increase speed and efficiency of such analysis. We suggest that these mid-term steps should focus on higher risk areas (i.e., most prolific past submarine landslide occurrence balanced with socio-economic factors such as proximity to population centers, seasonal touristic areas, and/or critical coastal and marine infrastructure). The recent advent of AI technology will lead to significant im- provements in computation capability in all areas of science. AI tech- niques are currently very effective only in mainstream applications, such as generative AI in image, video and text generation, or image classifi- cation to mention a few. Geoscience comparatively speaking is still a niche where big data is not predominantly available. However, we will inevitably see more and more AI models appearing in geoscience (and other science areas). We assume that there will be a trust hurdle that will have to be overcome. Once technologies are better understood and available, we anticipate its seamlessly integration with science appli- cations with potential for huge improvements in our ability to simulate, interpret and generate complex and large data sets quickly and reliably. This shift will likely transform hazard and risk assessment studies from a high-performance computing application, run by experts over weeks and months, into applications available to laymen organizations (in- surance companies, councils etc.)

Once technologies are better understood and available, we anticipate its seamlessly integration with science appli- cations with potential for huge improvements in our ability to simulate, interpret and generate complex and large data sets quickly and reliably. This shift will likely transform hazard and risk assessment studies from a high-performance computing application, run by experts over weeks and months, into applications available to laymen organizations (in- surance companies, councils etc.) and even individuals. Key to success in these areas is the use of modern data science, storage and delivery so- lutions which require technical expertise and domain knowledge at the same time. Close collaboration with domain trained data science teams is therefore also highly encouraged and science organizations need to invest in data science capabilities to be able to thrive in today’s data- driven landscape. Long-term (i.e., 10–20 years): (1) A strategic approach to new marine data acquisition; (2) enhanced sensor networks dedicated to monitoring and forewarning; and (3) entrain lessons learned from modern landslide- tsunami events. Fellow researchers are encouraged to prioritize and champion future data acquisition in areas with significant gaps in ba- thymetry data on continental margins and shelves. Conversely, areas where historic submarine landslides are identified, but not investigated in detail, can benefit from targeted acquisition of sub-surface geophys- ical information and sediment samples to provide age frameworks. Site specific studies on selected submarine landslides will continue to add value, especially if events are dated and tsunamigenic potential is investigated using source simulations. Ideally, such work will be com- plemented by further sub-regional to regional scale investigation of areas where multiple submarine landslides have occurred, providing detailed geophysical data, including sub-surface information, together with targeted sediment sampling to potentially date the event(s). Detection of submarine landslide processes is still in its infancy (Section 3.6) but ideally, will be widely expanded. This would serve dual pur- poses of firstly, providing increased direct evidence of real-time pro- cesses and their relationship to precursor and triggering events, and secondly will form the basis for future early warning systems. Ideally future role-out of such capability will be guided by the more immediate short and mid-term activities of identifying areas prone to submarine landslides and the area’s most at risk from resulting tsunami hazards. In tandem with these suggestions which focus on hazard and risk quantification from a preparatory, “pre-event” perspective, we must also continue to learn from real events and consider how to formulate optimal response to future events. We have seen that confirmed exam- ples of submarine landslide-tsunamis are rare, with few modern exam- ples presenting both landslide source information and tsunami observations/records to learn from (e.g., 1979 Nice, France: Assier-R- zadkieaicz et al., 2000, 1994 Skagway Harbor, Alaska: Kulikov et al., 1996, 2002 Stromboli, Italy: Tinti et al., 2005, 2018 Palu, Indonesia: Nakata et al., 2020). There is however an opportunity to learn from smaller scale more frequent events, for example, those that occur in freshwater bodies such as fjords and lakes. There is however an opportunity to learn from smaller scale more frequent events, for example, those that occur in freshwater bodies such as fjords and lakes. A recent New Zealand example is the November 2022 Lake Taup¯o earthquake, subaqueous landslide and tsunami which brings an unva- luable opportunity to study the processes from the landslide initiation to the tsunami generation, propagation, and inundation. On November 30, 2022, at night and close to a population center, a magnitude Mw 5.7 earthquake occurred in Lake Taup¯o, New Zealand’s largest lake, with a hypocenter located in the region of the Horomatangi Reef, in the eastern part of the lake (Fig. 10). Numerous terrestrial landslides were associ- ated with the shaking, and it was also followed by a small tsunami washing lines of pumice along the east and north shores and knocking over a few small boats around Wharewaka Point, a popular public beach, where the run-up distance was estimated around 42 m (Power et al., in prep). In fact, part of the beach and car park disappeared un- derwater in what appeared to be a rotational slump (identified via dives and a bathymetric survey). The very localized pattern of the tsunami with a pronounced run-up distance coincident with the location of the underwater landslide supports the hypothesis of tsunami generation locally by this landslide component, in addition to the earthquake component. Work is in progress to reproduce the tsunami records and validate the numerical model for tsunami generation with landslide source. Many data were obtained in real-time for scientists to quickly assess the event because the Taup¯o Lake Region is highly monitored owing to a history of cataclysmic past eruptions including the largest on Earth within the last 5 ka (e.g., Wilson, 2001). A dense network of sensors takes continuous readings via seismometers, GNSS stations, and hy- drothermal fluid and gas vents analysis. In addition, there are also three lake level monitoring gauges which recorded the November 2022 tsunami with the largest amplitude recorded on the gauge facing Wharewaka Point (Fig. 10). The event attracted significant media attention and public interest. The fact that this event occurred during the night with no casualties enabled the response effort to focus on science understanding, instead of disaster relief procedures. The science response involved numerous people with different backgrounds and skills and provides an excellent example of a cohesive and well managed science response involving several agencies across science, indigenous leaders, emergency management and local government agencies. Lake Taup¯o demonstrates that we can formulate a rapid, well- managed response for a relatively small area (lake surface is ~616 km2) in a highly monitored setting. Lake Taup¯o demonstrates that we can formulate a rapid, well- managed response for a relatively small area (lake surface is ~616 km2) in a highly monitored setting. Transposing the lessons learned and methodologies applied to a larger submarine scenario in an open marine setting however is not straightforward, for several reasons: (1) the entire New Zealand coast is only sparsely instrumented by 18 operational coastal gauges transmitting real-time data, (in addition to 12 DART stations monitoring subduction zones more than 100 km away from the J.H.M. Roger et al. Marine and Petroleum Geology 162 (2024) 106729 16 coasts); (2) the area mobilized by the landslide could be much larger, occur in a wide variety of water depths and be difficult and very resource expensive to locate after the fact; and (3), the area impacted by the resulting tsunami could be much larger, potentially impacting coastal communities and infrastructure and necessitating disaster response measures that would take precedence over immediate science understanding. For optimal preparedness, a systematic and high-resolution bathy- metric coverage of shallow coastal zones (i.e., <50 m) and continental margins should be pursued (high resolution bathymetric data for New Zealand shallow coastal zones stands at only 30 % coverage). Additional offshore and coastal sea-level gauges should be added to the current networks to densify the instrumental coverage. Offshore gauges could be additional DART-like sensors in very specific locations where a high- degree of landslide susceptibility exists.
